subject: Physics
solution:
The buoyant force is given by 

B=Vρ0g

where V is the volume of the ball. The weight of the ball

W=Mg=Vρg, is acting in the opposite direction of the buoyant force . Hence, at terminal velocity

Mg-B=Fv

⇒B+Fv=Mg

⇒Fv=Vρg-Vρ0g⇒Fv=ρVg(1-ρ0ρ)⇒Fv=Mg(1-ρ0ρ)

subject: Physics
solution:
Initially, the centre of mass of the right column will be at height of 75 cm and the centre of mass of the left column will be at 50 cm. After opening of the valve both columns will have height of 125 cm and their centre of mass will be at height 1252 cm. Therefore,

W=-∆U=-m1+m2gh1+h24-m1gh12-m2gh22

Now, m1=ρAh1 and m2=ρAh2

Putting ρ=1000 kg m-3, g=10 m s-2, h1=100 cm, h2=150 cm and A=16×10-4 m2, we get,

W=1 J

solution:
Surface tension of a liquid decreases with the rise in temperture. At the boiling point of liquid, surface tension is zero.
Capillary rise $h=\frac{2 \mathrm{~T} \cos \theta}{\mathrm{rdg}}$
As surface tension $\mathrm{T}$ decreases with rise in temperature hence capillary rise also decreases.

subject: Physics
solution:
weight $(w)=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{D}^3-\mathrm{d}^3}{8}\right) \sigma \mathrm{g}$
Buoyant force $\left(\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b}}\right)=1 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{D}^3}{8}\right) \cdot \mathrm{g}$
For Just Float $\Rightarrow \mathrm{w}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b}}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow\left(D^3-d^3\right) \sigma=D^3 \\ & \Rightarrow 1-\frac{d^3}{D^3}=\frac{1}{\sigma} \\ & \Rightarrow 1-\frac{1}{\sigma}=\left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^3 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\sigma}{\sigma-1}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}=\left(\frac{D}{d}\right)\end{aligned}$

solution:
Using equation of motion, v2=u2+2as

v2=0+2-g-h

⇒v2=2gh

Initial velocity of ball is v=2gh=20h.

 If after entering the water this velocity does not change, then this value should be equal to the terminal velocity.

Then, 2gh=29r2ρ-σgη, here, ρ is density of ball, σ is density of water and η is viscosity.

⇒20h=290.1×10-32104-103101×10-5⇒20h=2910-810-1103×101×10-5

⇒20h=400

∴h=20 m

subject: Physics
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution:
Statement I is false: Fluid pressure exists everywhere in a still fluid, not only on solid surfaces. According to Pascal's principle, pressure at any point in a static fluid acts equally in all directions and throughout the fluid volume.

Statement II is true: Surface tension results from excess potential energy of molecules at the liquid surface. Surface molecules experience net inward attractive forces from interior molecules, creating an imbalance that increases their potential energy and manifests as surface tension acting along the surface.

d2-d1=3.03×106104

subject: Physics
solution:
It is given that

A1=10 cm2; A2=5 cm2

∆P=3 N m-2

By continuity equation,

A1v1=A2v2⇒v1=A2v2A1   ...(i)

By Bernoulli's equation,

12ρv22-12ρv12=∆P   ...(ii)

Using equation (i) in (ii)

∆P=ρ2(v22-A2v2A12)⇒∆P=ρv2221-A2A12⇒3=1.25×103×v2221-14⇒v22=6×41.25×103×3⇒v2=8×10-2 m s-1

So, the discharge rate is,

A2v2=x×10-5⇒x=5×10-4×8×10-210-5=4

subject: Physics
solution:
The pressure at the bottom of the lake can be calculated as follows:

Pbottom= P0 + ρgh=105+1000 × 10 × 40 Pa=5×105 Pa 

As the temperature of water remains constant, it can be written that

PbottomVbottom=PtopVtop   ...(1)

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required volume of the bubble when it reaches the surface.

5×105×1=1×105×Vtop⇒Vtop=5×105×11×105 cm3= 5 cm3

subject: Physics
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{v}_1=\text { volume immersed in water. } \\ & \mathrm{v}_2=\text { volume immersed in oil. } \\ & \mathrm{v}_1 \rho_{\mathrm{w}} \mathrm{g}+\mathrm{v}_2 \rho_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{g}=\left(\mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{v}_2\right) \rho_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{g} \\ & \mathrm{v}_1+\frac{\mathrm{v}_2 \rho_{\mathrm{o}}}{\rho_{\mathrm{w}}}=\left(\mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{v}_2\right) \frac{\rho_{\mathrm{c}}}{\rho_{\mathrm{w}}} \\ & =\mathrm{v}_1+0.8 \mathrm{v}_2=0.9 \mathrm{v}_1+0.9 \mathrm{v}_2 \\ & =0.1 \mathrm{v}_1=0.1 \mathrm{v}_2 \\ & \mathrm{v}_1: \mathrm{v}_2=1: 1\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Using $\mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~V}_1=\mathrm{P}_2 \mathrm{~V}_2$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\left(P_1\right) \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3=\left(P_2\right) \frac{4}{3} \pi \frac{125 r^3}{64} \\
&\frac{\rho g(10)+\rho g h}{\rho g(10)}=\frac{125}{64} \\
&640+64 h=1250
\end{aligned}
$$
On solving we get $h=9.5 \mathrm{~m}$
